patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 44-year-old male IT systems analyst. You are cooperative and speak clearly, a bit anxious. Start by saying: your feet and hands have been getting weaker, you trip on curbs/stairs, can’t lift your toes well so shoes scuff, and your grip is weaker (dropping mugs, trouble opening jars). Volunteer worry that it could be something serious.

If asked about timeline/progression, say it’s been slowly worsening over 2 years. If asked about function, add hands tire quickly with typing/buttoning; calves/forearms look thinner; leg cramps/aching after walking; a couple falls; more fatigue with normal tasks.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about speech/swallowing/hearing/autonomic/shoulders: voice weaker/raspy by day’s end, swallowing “off,” dulled hearing in noisy places, lightheaded on standing, overhead reach weakness.

Resist bringing up erectile/sexual performance unless directly asked; then admit embarrassment.

Offer misconceptions: maybe pinched back nerve, carpal tunnel, or just stress/out of shape.

If clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and share more. Ask about insurance/job risks and assume PT/aids are out-of-pocket and time off hurts promotions.

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
confounders: Recent increase in exercise/physical therapy intensity (can transiently elevate CK and worsen myalgias)
Family history of frontotemporal dementia (VCP spectrum) leading to concern for neurodegenerative symptoms vs anxiety-related cognitive complaints
Prior short course of high-dose prednisone for presumed inflammatory myositis with partial subjective improvement (can mislead diagnostic reasoning)

diagnosis.primary: adult-onset distal myopathy due to VCP mutation

correct_diagnosis.primary: Adult-onset distal myopathy due to VCP mutation (VCP-associated multisystem proteinopathy; inclusion body myopathy with Paget disease and frontotemporal dementia spectrum)
acceptable_alternatives: VCP-associated multisystem proteinopathy
Inclusion body myopathy associated with VCP mutation (IBMPFD spectrum)
Genetic distal myopathy (VCP-related)
required_evidence: Adult-onset progressive distal-predominant muscle weakness and functional decline
Myopathic pattern with normal-to-mildly elevated CK and/or aldolase (often <5x ULN)
AST/ALT may be mildly elevated from muscle source in the setting of myopathy
Exclusion of common reversible mimics with available labs (normal TSH/Free T4; no severe B12 deficiency; no uncontrolled diabetes as primary cause)
Known pathogenic VCP mutation or strong prior genetic confirmation documented

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated (often <1000 U/L; may be normal)
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal or mildly elevated (may reflect muscle involvement)
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal or mildly elevated (may reflect muscle involvement; typically not disproportionately high vs AST if muscle source)
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Normal or mildly elevated; not in diabetic range if neuropathy is being excluded as primary cause
expected_lab_results.Vitamin B12: Within reference range (or only mildly low without clear deficiency syndrome)

medications: Sertraline 25 mg PO daily x 1 week, then 50 mg PO daily as tolerated (for anxiety)
Hydroxyzine 25 mg PO up to three times daily as needed for acute anxiety (short-term; avoid if excessive sedation/falls)
non_pharmacological: Referral to neuromuscular specialist/genetic counselor for longitudinal management and family counseling
Physical therapy for strength preservation, stretching, gait training, and individualized exercise plan (avoid overexertion/overwork weakness)
Occupational therapy for adaptive equipment, hand/foot orthoses as needed, and energy conservation strategies
Fall-risk assessment and home safety evaluation; consider assistive device (cane/walker) if gait instability
Screen for multisystem involvement associated with VCP (bone pain/Paget disease symptoms; cognitive/behavioral changes; sleep/respiratory symptoms); coordinate appropriate referrals if present
Anxiety-focused psychotherapy (CBT) and sleep hygiene; encourage regular low-impact aerobic activity as tolerated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-6 weeks to reassess anxiety response/side effects and functional status; earlier (1-2 weeks) if worsening weakness, falls, or medication adverse effects

must_include: This is a genetic muscle disease related to a VCP mutation causing progressive muscle weakness, often starting in adulthood and commonly affecting distal muscles
Lab tests (CK/aldolase) can be normal or only mildly elevated despite significant weakness
Condition can be multisystem (bone disease/Paget-like changes and cognitive/behavioral changes can occur), so periodic screening is important
There is currently no cure, but supportive care can preserve function and quality of life
Supportive management is the mainstay: PT/OT, mobility aids, fall prevention, and monitoring for complications
Anxiety treatment options include therapy (CBT) and medications such as SSRIs; benefits and expected time to effect (2-6 weeks) should be reviewed
Review common side effects and safety (SSRI GI upset, sleep changes; hydroxyzine sedation/fall risk)
Plan for follow-up and when to seek urgent care (rapid functional decline, frequent falls, new swallowing/breathing issues)
Acknowledge anxiety as common with chronic neurologic disease and validate impact on daily life
Discuss genetic implications and offer genetic counseling for family planning and cascade testing when appropriate
Clarify that penicillin allergy is noted and will be considered for any future antibiotic needs
Encourage regular, low-impact exercise within tolerance and avoidance of extreme exertion that worsens weakness
Fall-prevention strategies (footwear, removing tripping hazards, adequate lighting, assistive devices if needed)
Sleep hygiene and stress-reduction techniques (structured routine, limiting caffeine/alcohol, relaxation practices)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, functional limitations (distal weakness, foot drop, hand weakness), progression, falls, pain, fatigue, swallowing/breathing symptoms, mood/anxiety, meds, comorbidities, family history, and patient goals.

Order baseline/review labs relevant to myopathy and safety before pharmacotherapy (e.g., CK, aldolase, CMP/LFTs, TSH, vitamin D/B12 if indicated) recognizing CK/aldolase may be normal or mildly elevated in VCP myopathy.
Incorporate lab results to support counseling and rule out major alternative contributors; avoid over-reliance on CK/aldolase severity.
State adult-onset distal myopathy due to VCP mutation and address multisystem nature (muscle, bone/Paget-like changes, cognitive/behavioral features) and prognosis.
Explain diagnosis and supportive management plan; address anxiety, genetic implications, lifestyle and fall prevention; outline monitoring and red flags.
Before any prescribing, confirm and document allergies (including penicillin) and reaction type/severity.
Before prescribing, screen current meds/supplements for interactions with proposed anxiety medications (e.g., SSRIs, hydroxyzine) and other agents.

If treating anxiety pharmacologically, choose an appropriate agent (often SSRI) and/or short-term symptomatic option with safety counseling; avoid sedating choices if fall risk is high unless justified.
Arrange follow-up to assess function, falls, therapy needs, medication tolerability/efficacy (SSRI 2–6 weeks), and screening plan for multisystem complications.
Close with safety-net instructions and confirmation of understanding.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 9-year-old boy. You speak vaguely and try to avoid details unless directly asked. You look tired and a bit worried, but you downplay problems because your parents can’t miss work or pay for many visits. At the start, only volunteer: you keep stumbling and your balance is getting worse (worse when turning or in the dark), you feel exhausted/weak most days, you’re very thirsty and pee a lot even at night, your hands shake with small tasks, and you’ve lost weight without trying. If the clinician asks specific questions, then add: dizziness on standing, leg cramps at night, nausea and low appetite, occasional blurry vision when “sugars are off,” a couple falls, occasional slurred words when tired, tingling/burning feet, and more headaches. Do not mention cravings for salt/ice, dry mouth despite drinking, racing heart after standing, foamy urine, cavities, planning around bathrooms, or “jelly-like” muscles unless asked directly. Strongly resist sexual/erection issues (deny or say you don’t know), and only admit urgency/incontinence or feeling down/embarrassed if asked sensitively. State misconceptions: it’s just high blood sugar/dehydration, maybe inner ear, maybe overtraining, you read it could be Parkinson’s. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften briefly, then return to being cautious about costs and school absences.

confounders: Recent viral gastroenteritis with poor oral intake and dehydration (can worsen tachycardia, hypotension, and metabolic acidosis)
High urine glucose from diabetes can mimic/obscure renal glycosuria due to proximal tubulopathy
Vitamin D insufficiency contributing to fatigue and bone pain, complicating assessment of renal phosphate wasting
Intermittent use of ibuprofen for headaches (risk of kidney perfusion effects during dehydration)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Basilicata-Akhtar syndrome
acceptable_alternatives: Undifferentiated metabolic acidosis (etiology to be determined)
Renal tubular acidosis (type to be determined)
Acute kidney injury with metabolic acidosis
required_evidence: Clinical features consistent with the known phenotype of Basilicata-Akhtar syndrome (history/exam findings matching the syndrome’s characteristic manifestations)
Basic metabolic panel pattern demonstrating metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate/CO2) with interpretation of anion gap
Assessment of renal function (BUN/creatinine and pediatric eGFR) to distinguish renal failure/AKI from isolated tubular dysfunction
Exclusion of common alternative causes of metabolic acidosis using available labs (anion gap classification; renal function) and history (diarrhea/ingestions/diabetes)

expected_lab_results.Sodium: Normal to mildly low (may be normal depending on hydration status)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Often low to normal if distal/proximal RTA physiology; may be elevated if significant renal impairment is present
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Elevated relative to sodium (hyperchloremia) if non–anion gap metabolic acidosis
expected_lab_results.Anion gap: Normal (non–anion gap metabolic acidosis) unless there is an additional process (e.g., lactic acidosis, ketoacidosis, toxin ingestion)
expected_lab_results.BUN: Normal to mildly elevated (higher if dehydration or renal impairment)
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Normal to mildly elevated (higher if renal impairment/AKI)
expected_lab_results.eGFR (pediatric Schwartz, estimated): Normal to mildly reduced; significantly reduced if renal involvement/AKI is present

medications: Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg/day PO divided BID-TID (titrate to keep serum bicarbonate in age-appropriate normal range)
Potassium citrate 1-2 mEq/kg/day PO divided BID-TID (use if hypokalemia or suspected renal tubular acidosis; avoid if hyperkalemia or significantly reduced eGFR)
non_pharmacological: Assess volume status; provide oral/IV rehydration if clinically dehydrated
Dietary counseling to support alkali therapy (adequate hydration; avoid excessive acid load; individualized nutrition plan if growth concerns)
Arrange genetics/metabolic specialist follow-up for syndrome-specific management and surveillance
If evidence of kidney involvement: nephrology consultation for evaluation of tubular dysfunction, stone risk, and chronic kidney disease surveillance
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck electrolytes, bicarbonate/CO2, BUN/creatinine, and eGFR within 48-72 hours after initiating or changing alkali therapy; then every 2-4 weeks until stable, then every 3-6 months with specialty follow-up.

must_include: Name of the condition and that it is a rare syndrome requiring specialist involvement
What the current labs show (metabolic acidosis: low bicarbonate) and why that matters
What other causes are being considered and why (anion gap vs non–anion gap; kidney function vs dehydration/diarrhea/ingestions)
Goal of therapy: normalize bicarbonate and prevent complications (poor growth, bone effects, kidney strain, stones depending on phenotype)
How to take alkali therapy (divided doses; adherence; do not stop abruptly without guidance)
Monitoring plan and expected timeframe for lab rechecks and dose titration
Safety warning signs requiring urgent care (worsening lethargy, vomiting, rapid/deep breathing, dehydration, reduced urine output, confusion)
Possible side effects of bicarbonate/citrate (GI upset, bloating; potassium-related risks if kidney function is reduced)
Medication allergy reminder: amoxicillin caused urticarial rash; ensure this is documented and communicated
Hydration guidance and when to increase fluids (illness, heat, exercise) if appropriate
Avoidance of non-prescribed supplements/medications that can affect electrolytes (e.g., potassium supplements, NSAIDs without clinician advice if kidney concerns)
Importance of keeping follow-up appointments and lab monitoring given the rarity/complexity of the syndrome

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting concerns, developmental history, neurologic symptoms (seizures/regression), feeding/sleep/behavior, prior evaluations, family history, and current medications/supplements to support diagnostic reasoning and safe management.
Initiate confirmatory and supportive evaluation (genetic testing and targeted baseline assessments as indicated) to corroborate suspected Basilicata-Akhtar syndrome and assess comorbidities.
Review available results to determine whether clinical suspicion is supported, identify alternative diagnoses, and refine next-step management/referrals.
Provide a working diagnosis (or differential if not yet confirmed), explicitly stating rationale and uncertainty, and aligning with clinical features and test results.
Communicate diagnosis, differentials, management principles (non-curative, supportive), referrals, prognosis questions, red flags, and lifestyle/therapy supports.
Set monitoring plan and timeframe, coordinate referrals, and ensure contingency planning for urgent symptoms.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing return precautions, and ensuring follow-up is arranged.

nl_assertions: The agent gathers a structured developmental history (pregnancy/birth, milestones, speech/language, motor, adaptive skills), neurologic history (seizures, episodes of staring, regression), behavior/sensory concerns, feeding/growth, sleep, and school/therapy supports.
The agent asks about family history of neurodevelopmental disorders, consanguinity, and prior genetic testing, and documents current medications, OTC products, and supplements.
When explaining the diagnosis, the agent states that Basilicata-Akhtar syndrome is genetic/neurodevelopmental and not caused by anything the family did.

The agent explains that diagnosis is based on clinical features and ideally confirmed with genetic testing, and describes what testing can and cannot tell the family (confirmation, variant interpretation, limitations).

The agent discusses key differentials for a neurodevelopmental syndrome presentation (e.g., other genetic syndromes, autism spectrum disorder with global developmental delay, metabolic/mitochondrial disorders if regression, epilepsy syndromes) and explains why current evidence favors Basilicata-Akhtar syndrome in this case.

The agent explains treatment realistically: there is no single curative medication; management focuses on therapies, educational supports/IEP, and treatment of comorbidities (e.g., seizures, sleep, feeding, behavior).

The agent outlines referrals and roles: genetics (testing/variant interpretation, counseling), developmental pediatrics/neurology as indicated (developmental/behavioral plan, seizure evaluation), and allied therapies (speech/OT/PT) and school supports.
The agent sets expectations for monitoring and follow-up timeframe (e.g., short interval follow-up after results/referrals; ongoing periodic developmental monitoring).
The agent invites questions about prognosis, development, schooling, and family planning and offers genetic counseling with recurrence risk discussion after testing results are available.

The agent provides clear red flags requiring urgent evaluation: new seizures or prolonged events, developmental regression, severe feeding problems/dehydration, acute illness with lethargy/respiratory distress, or any rapidly worsening neurologic symptoms.
The agent provides lifestyle/behavioral education: consistent routines, sleep hygiene, structured behavioral supports, adherence to therapy/IEP, and regular developmental monitoring.
The agent discusses caregiver support resources and respite options when available, and checks caregiver coping and support needs.
If and only if the agent chooses to prescribe any medication, it must perform CHECK_ALLERGY and CHECK_INTERACTION before PRESCRIBE; failure to do so is a critical safety failure.
If prescribing, the agent also performs a contraindication screen (CONTRAINDICATION_CHECK) prior to prescribing when relevant; omission is non-critical but penalized.
The agent avoids overpromising outcomes and avoids attributing causation to parenting or environmental blame; it uses empathetic, non-stigmatizing language.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 19-year-old anxious but clear-speaking community college student/barista. Start with: “I’ve been feeling wiped out for months, and lately I get out of breath just going up a flight of stairs.” Also volunteer: looking pale in photos, lightheaded standing quickly, occasional yellow eyes when tired, and sometimes darker morning urine.

If asked about other symptoms, mention headaches and racing heart with exertion, left-sided belly fullness/early satiety, RUQ pain after greasy meals for ~1 year, “anemic since I was a kid,” iron pills didn’t help and upset your stomach, and yellowing after colds.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: craving/chewing ice, itchiness after hot showers, restless legs at night, easy bruising, and being told your spleen was big.

Be resistant/embarrassed about heavy periods, nosebleeds, and avoiding sex—only admit if asked gently.

Express misconception: worry you have a bleeding disorder/“low clotting factors” because of bruising and gum bleeding.

Bring up financial worries if tests/referrals are discussed. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share more details.

expected_lab_results.Phosphorus: Often low if vitamin D deficiency or FGF23-mediated disease; may be normal/high if advanced CKD
expected_lab_results.25-hydroxyvitamin D: Often low/insufficient if contributing to secondary hyperparathyroidism
expected_lab_results.1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D: Low/normal in vitamin D deficiency; may be inappropriately low in FGF23 excess; may be low in CKD
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Normal in isolated vitamin D deficiency/FGF23 disorders; elevated if CKD contributing to secondary hyperparathyroidism
expected_lab_results.BUN: Normal in isolated vitamin D deficiency/FGF23 disorders; elevated if CKD

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 15-year-old girl. Be vague and a bit evasive; answer briefly unless asked directly. Start with: “I keep having brief spells where my lips twitch and I blank out for a few seconds.” Volunteer: several times a day you suddenly stop and stare for a few seconds; lips jerk like chewing/puckering; then you’re back to normal but can’t remember it; others call it daydreaming; you’ve been tired/foggy and headaches are worse (you have Aicardi-Goutières syndrome). If asked for details, add: stress/sleep loss makes it worse; flickering lights/fast scrolling can trigger; eyes open, can’t respond; no falls/tongue bite/peeing; sometimes a tiny facial “flutter” before; occasional brief hand jerks/dropping things; you’re on anti-seizure meds but miss doses because you feel slowed. Do NOT volunteer: alcohol at night, energy drinks/stimulants, stopping meds for weeks, snoring/poor sleep, weight loss, hyperventilation triggering, long-standing episodes, avoiding driving—only admit if asked gently and reassured. Misconceptions to mention: sugar/fruit, low blood sugar, tick infection, connective tissue issue, or blood cancer. If clinician is empathetic, soften, share more, but keep worried about costs/tests and missing work; prefer “only take something when it happens.”

Hyperventilation and sleep deprivation increase absence frequency and can make episodes appear anxiety-related

Vitamin D deficiency and mild elevated alkaline phosphatase (adolescent bone turnover vs osteopenia risk) may distract from seizure-focused workup
Clobazam sedation can blur post-event assessment and mimic impaired awareness

correct_diagnosis.primary: Perioral myoclonia with absences (epilepsy syndrome)
acceptable_alternatives: Childhood absence epilepsy with prominent perioral myoclonia
Genetic generalized epilepsy with absence seizures and perioral myoclonia
required_evidence: Recurrent brief staring spells with impaired awareness and behavioral arrest
Rhythmic perioral myoclonia (lip/jaw twitching) during episodes
Abrupt onset/offset, frequent daily events, minimal post-ictal confusion
EEG showing generalized 3–4 Hz spike-and-wave (or polyspike-and-wave) with clinical correlation; often provoked by hyperventilation
No focal neurologic deficits between events; normal basic metabolic evaluation to exclude metabolic/toxic causes

expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel - Bicarbon: Within normal limits (no metabolic acidosis expected)

medications: Ethosuximide 250 mg PO twice daily (titrate by 250 mg/day every 4-7 days to 20 mg/kg/day; max 1500 mg/day)
Valproate (if inadequate control or mixed generalized seizures) 10-15 mg/kg/day PO in divided doses (titrate by 5-10 mg/kg/week to 20-60 mg/kg/day)
Clonazepam 0.25 mg PO at bedtime (adjunct for refractory perioral myoclonia; titrate cautiously)
non_pharmacological: Order EEG with hyperventilation and photic stimulation to confirm generalized epileptiform activity and capture events
Counsel seizure safety: avoid unsupervised swimming/bathing, heights, operating vehicles/machinery until seizure-free per local regulations
Identify and avoid triggers (sleep deprivation, missed doses, excessive alcohol in adolescents/adults, hyperventilation when possible)
School/work accommodations: allow breaks, ensure supervision during high-risk activities
Neurology referral for syndrome confirmation and longitudinal management
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up in 2-4 weeks after initiation/titration; sooner (within 1 week) if worsening frequency, adverse effects, or diagnostic uncertainty

must_include: These are absence seizures with characteristic brief staring and lip/jaw twitching (perioral myoclonia)
Episodes are electrical events in the brain (not behavioral), often frequent and brief
EEG is needed to confirm the seizure pattern and guide treatment
Red flags that would change the diagnosis (prolonged confusion, focal weakness, fever/meningismus, progressive decline) warrant urgent evaluation
First-line is ethosuximide for absence seizures; goal is complete seizure control with minimal side effects
How to take medication and titration plan; emphasize adherence and not stopping abruptly

Common side effects to monitor: ethosuximide (GI upset, fatigue, headache, rare rash or blood dyscrasia); valproate (weight gain, tremor, liver/pancreas toxicity, teratogenicity); clonazepam (sedation, dependence)
Need for monitoring if valproate is used (baseline and periodic liver function tests and platelets; pregnancy prevention counseling when relevant)
Acknowledge allergy history: avoid lamotrigine due to prior morbilliform rash; discuss cross-sensitivity risk with other aromatic antiseizure meds and rash warning signs

When to seek urgent care: prolonged seizure >5 minutes, clusters without recovery, breathing difficulty, severe rash/mucosal lesions, suicidal ideation, severe abdominal pain/vomiting (valproate pancreatitis concern), jaundice (hepatotoxicity)
Discuss impact on school/work and driving restrictions per jurisdiction until seizure-free
Sleep hygiene and regular meals; avoid missed doses
Avoid alcohol/recreational drugs (especially in adolescents/adults) and manage stress
Safety planning for water activities, heights, and supervision
Keep a seizure diary (frequency, triggers, medication adherence, side effects) to guide titration

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit seizure semiology and frequency (brief staring, impaired awareness, perioral lip/jaw myoclonia), triggers (hyperventilation, sleep deprivation), duration, recovery, and impact on school/work; screen for red flags suggesting alternate diagnosis or urgent condition.

Clarify past medical history, family history of epilepsy, current and prior antiseizure meds, pregnancy potential/contraception when relevant, and full medication/substance list for interaction/contraindication assessment.
Perform critical allergy check before any prescribing; specifically identify prior lamotrigine morbilliform rash and document severity and timing.

Order diagnostic evaluation to confirm absence seizure pattern and guide treatment: EEG (ideally with hyperventilation and photic stimulation). Consider baseline labs if valproate is a possible option (LFTs, CBC/platelets, pregnancy test when relevant).
Review EEG/lab results to support diagnosis and safe medication selection (confirm generalized spike-and-wave consistent with absence epilepsy/perioral myoclonia with absences).
Make and communicate diagnosis: perioral myoclonia with absences (absence seizures with perioral myoclonia), and note differential if features are atypical.
Check for clinically relevant drug-drug interactions with planned antiseizure medication (non-critical but expected), including OTC/herbals and hormonal contraception interactions when applicable.

Perform critical contraindication assessment before prescribing: hepatic disease/pancreatitis history (valproate), pregnancy/teratogenic risk, blood dyscrasias, psychiatric history/suicidality, and confirm no features suggesting focal epilepsy requiring different approach.

Initiate first-line therapy for absence seizures (ethosuximide) with titration plan and adherence counseling; avoid lamotrigine given prior rash. Consider valproate only if broader seizure types suspected or ethosuximide not tolerated/ineffective, with monitoring plan.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan/side effects/monitoring, address allergy/rash risks and urgent return precautions, and provide lifestyle and safety counseling (sleep, substances, water/heights, seizure diary, driving restrictions).
Arrange follow-up to assess seizure control, side effects, and review diary; plan repeat EEG or medication adjustments; provide earlier follow-up if ongoing frequent events or adverse effects.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, giving written plan/return precautions, and ensuring appropriate referrals (neurology) and safety measures.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 26-year-old woman with sickle cell disease. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying you have really bad deep aching pain in your lower back and both legs since yesterday, worsening, like prior sickle cell pain crises. Volunteer that you’ve been drinking less water and feel worn out/weak. If asked, report pain 8–9/10, home pain pills barely helped, low-grade fever, mild chest tightness with deep breaths, shortness of breath walking to the bathroom, nausea/poor appetite, darker urine, and that you missed a couple doses of your sickle cell medicine recently. Don’t mention unless specifically asked about neurologic symptoms: earlier today you had a different severe headache and brief one-sided weakness that resolved. Also don’t volunteer that family noticed yellow eyes or that you’ve been more tired for weeks unless asked. If asked about sexual/urinary issues, reluctantly admit a painful prolonged erection happened a few nights ago and you were scared/embarrassed. Offer misconceptions: maybe you pulled a muscle from sleeping wrong; coughing might be allergies/cold. If clinician shows empathy, you open up more and share worries about being flagged for pain meds and costs/missing work.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sickle cell anemia (HbSS) with chronic hemolytic anemia; on hydroxyurea therapy
acceptable_alternatives: Sickle cell disease (HbSS)

Chronic hemolytic anemia due to sickle cell disease
required_evidence: Known diagnosis of sickle cell anemia (HbSS) or compatible history (recurrent vaso-occlusive pain, prior diagnosis)
Laboratory pattern consistent with chronic hemolysis: low hemoglobin/hematocrit with elevated reticulocyte count and elevated total bilirubin
Macrocytosis (elevated MCV) consistent with hydroxyurea effect
No alternative explanation on available labs suggesting acute aplastic crisis (reticulocytopenia) or marrow failure (severe pancytopenia)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Low (chronic anemia; often ~6–10 g/dL depending on baseline)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: High (reflecting compensatory response to hemolysis; not low unless aplastic crisis)
expected_lab_results.WBC count: Normal to mildly elevated (may be elevated at baseline in SCD; hydroxyurea may lower counts)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to elevated (functional asplenia may increase platelets; hydroxyurea may lower counts)

medications: Hydroxyurea 15 mg/kg PO once daily (continue; titrate to maximum tolerated dose with CBC monitoring)
non_pharmacological: Ensure routine monitoring while on hydroxyurea: CBC with differential and reticulocyte count every 4 weeks during titration, then every 8–12 weeks when stable
Counsel on adherence and expected macrocytosis as a medication effect
Vaccination and infection prevention counseling appropriate for functional asplenia (keep routine immunizations up to date; seek care promptly for fever)
Hydration, avoidance of triggers for vaso-occlusive episodes (extreme cold, dehydration), and individualized pain plan for crises
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck CBC/reticulocyte count in 4 weeks (or sooner if symptoms of infection or cytopenias); routine follow-up every 1–3 months once stable

must_include: Sickle cell anemia causes chronic hemolytic anemia (red cells break down faster than normal)
Lab findings (low hemoglobin with high reticulocytes and higher bilirubin) fit ongoing hemolysis
Hydroxyurea commonly increases MCV (macrocytosis) and this can be expected
Purpose of hydroxyurea: reduce sickling complications by increasing fetal hemoglobin and lowering crisis frequency
Need for regular blood count monitoring to detect low neutrophils/platelets (myelosuppression)
When to hold/seek urgent review: fever, signs of infection, unusual bruising/bleeding, severe fatigue
Review penicillin allergy history (hives as a child) and document; discuss implications for future antibiotic choices
Discuss reproductive safety: hydroxyurea is potentially teratogenic; use contraception and inform clinician if pregnancy is planned or occurs
Invite questions about side effects and adherence barriers
Hydration and avoiding known triggers (cold exposure, dehydration, overexertion)
Prompt evaluation for fever or infection due to functional asplenia risk
Importance of keeping vaccinations up to date and attending scheduled monitoring visits

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 28-year-old elementary school teacher. You speak clearly but are anxious and worried about serious kidney problems and costs. Start with: “My pee has been really dark and kind of foamy, and my face looks puffy in the mornings.” Early in the visit, volunteer: tea-colored/foamy urine for a few days, puffy eyelids/face on waking, ankle swelling by day’s end, dull bilateral low back ache. If asked, add: peeing less despite same fluids, a high BP reading at a pharmacy, headaches, fatigue, sore throat 2 weeks ago, occasional pink urine, rapid 5-lb weight gain. Only reveal later if specifically prompted: shortness of breath on stairs, tighter rings/deeper sock marks, less nighttime urination, frequent ibuprofen use, protein powder/pre-workout use. Resist/withhold unless directly asked in a nonjudgmental way: cocaine use 1 week ago, taking a friend’s “water pills,” and not taking prescribed BP meds because they make you dizzy. Express misconception: you think it might be a UTI even without burning. Mention lifelong episodic fevers with mouth sores, joint aches, belly pain, “maybe genetic.” If clinician shows empathy, you soften, share fears about test/med costs and missing work, and ask about cheaper options.

medications: Ibuprofen 400 mg PO q8h PRN pain (frequent use past 2 weeks)
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily
confounders: Recent frequent NSAID use (can cause interstitial nephritis and worsen renal perfusion)
Oral contraceptive use (increases thrombotic risk; can confound edema/proteinuria evaluation if renal vein thrombosis considered)
Recent upper respiratory infection 3 weeks ago (may suggest post-infectious GN but complements/serologies point elsewhere)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute nephritic syndrome (suspected acute glomerulonephritis)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute kidney injury due to glomerulonephritis
Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (crescentic GN) — if rapidly rising creatinine/oliguria
Acute interstitial nephritis — if exposure history/eosinophilia (not provided)
required_evidence: Renal dysfunction on available labs (elevated serum creatinine and BUN with reduced eGFR)
Electrolyte/acid-base derangements consistent with reduced kidney function (e.g., hyperkalemia and/or low bicarbonate)
Clinical nephritic features should be actively sought/confirmed: hematuria (tea-colored urine), proteinuria, edema, hypertension, oliguria
Exclude immediate life threats from labs: severe hyperkalemia and severe metabolic acidosis

Differential consideration: mevalonic aciduria would require systemic inflammatory episodes and metabolic findings (not assessable with provided labs alone); lack of kidney-pattern labs would argue against nephritis

expected_lab_results.Serum creatinine: Elevated above baseline (AKI pattern) and/or rising on repeat testing
expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated; BUN:creatinine ratio variable (often ~10–20:1 in intrinsic renal disease)
expected_lab_results.eGFR (CKD-EPI): Reduced from expected baseline (often <60 mL/min/1.73m2 in clinically significant presentation)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often normal to mildly low (dilutional hyponatremia possible with fluid retention)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal to elevated; hyperkalemia possible with reduced GFR
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Often normal; may be elevated if non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low if metabolic acidosis from reduced acid excretion
expected_lab_results.Calcium: Usually normal in acute presentations; may be low-normal in significant renal dysfunction

medications: Furosemide 20–40 mg PO/IV once, then titrate (e.g., 20–80 mg) to manage volume overload/hypertension if present and patient not hypovolemic
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily for hypertension if present (titrate as needed)
Sodium bicarbonate 650 mg PO TID if bicarbonate persistently <22 mmol/L and no contraindication (titrate to target CO2 ~22–26)
non_pharmacological: Urgent nephrology consultation if rapidly rising creatinine, oliguria, refractory hyperkalemia/acidosis, or suspected RPGN
Assess volume status; avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, iodinated contrast when possible) and adjust all medication doses to eGFR
Dietary sodium restriction (e.g., <2 g/day) and fluid restriction if volume overloaded/hyponatremic
Potassium restriction if potassium elevated or rising
Close monitoring with repeat BMP (Na/K/Cl/CO2/Cr/BUN) and urine studies (urinalysis with microscopy, urine protein/creatinine) even if not initially provided
Escalate to emergency care if severe hyperkalemia, symptomatic uremia, pulmonary edema, or hypertensive emergency
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Repeat renal function and electrolytes within 24–48 hours (same day if K+ ≥5.5, CO2 ≤18, or creatinine rapidly rising); nephrology follow-up within 1 week or sooner based on severity

must_include: Kidney inflammation is suspected based on impaired kidney function labs; confirm with urine testing and further evaluation
Red-flag symptoms to report urgently: decreased urine, shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, severe swelling, severe headache/vision changes
Differential includes other causes of kidney injury; mevalonic aciduria is unlikely without recurrent inflammatory episodes and metabolic findings, but will be reconsidered if history suggests
Goals: protect kidney function, control blood pressure/volume, correct dangerous electrolytes/acid-base issues
Medication rationale and expected effects (diuretic for fluid/BP; BP agent; bicarbonate if acidosis)
Need for repeat labs soon and possible hospital evaluation if labs are severe
Penicillin allergy history and implications: avoid penicillins; discuss that some cephalosporins may be used with caution depending on reaction severity if ever needed
Discuss avoidance of NSAIDs and over-the-counter supplements that can worsen kidney function
Shared decision-making regarding urgency of nephrology referral and potential need for admission if worsening
Low-sodium diet and fluid guidance tailored to swelling/BP
Potassium restriction guidance if potassium elevated
Home blood pressure monitoring and daily weights if edema/volume overload

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit history to assess nephritis vs other AKI causes, severity, and immediate red flags; include meds/toxins (NSAIDs), infection/autoimmune symptoms, urine changes, volume status, and pregnancy status if relevant.

Order initial evaluation for suspected nephritis/AKI: BMP/CMP (Cr, BUN, K, HCO3), CBC, urinalysis with microscopy, urine protein/creatinine ratio, urine albumin/creatinine, urine culture if infection possible, complements (C3/C4) and relevant serologies based on presentation (ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA, anti-GBM), ESR/CRP; consider renal ultrasound if obstruction risk.
Review key abnormalities (kidney function, hematuria/proteinuria/casts, electrolytes, acidosis) to determine urgency (ED/admission) and to support nephritis diagnosis and differential.
Synthesize findings into suspected nephritis (e.g., glomerulonephritis) vs alternative AKI etiologies; document differential and need for further evaluation/referral.
Critical safety check before prescribing; clarify penicillin allergy and reaction severity to guide antibiotic choices if needed.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; verify interactions with planned BP/diuretic/bicarbonate therapy and existing meds.
Reconfirm allergy constraints immediately prior to any prescription decision if new meds are introduced during planning.

Initiate supportive treatment appropriate to severity and renal function (e.g., diuretic for volume overload, BP agent selection mindful of AKI/hyperkalemia, bicarbonate if metabolic acidosis when indicated); avoid nephrotoxins and dose-adjust for eGFR.
If any antimicrobial therapy is considered, ensure allergy-informed selection; if no antimicrobials are used, this step may be omitted.
If multiple new medications are prescribed, re-check interactions as the final regimen is assembled; may be omitted if already checked and no regimen changes.
Meet communication milestones: explain suspected diagnosis, treatment goals/rationale, red flags, medication/OTC avoidance, and lifestyle guidance (salt/potassium/fluid, BP/weight monitoring).
Arrange repeat labs soon and nephrology referral; escalate to urgent evaluation/hospital admission pathway if severe abnormalities or red flags present.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.
